 {"id":"wa-985.3","title":"Analytics CLI: wa analytics summary/breakdown/export commands","description":"# Analytics CLI commands

## Purpose
Provide user-friendly CLI commands for viewing and exporting usage analytics.

## Commands

### wa analytics
Default summary view:
```bash
wa analytics

# Output
╭────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Usage Analytics (Last 7 Days)                          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Total Tokens: 1,234,567                                │
│ Estimated Cost: $12.34                                 │
│ Rate Limits Hit: 3                                     │
│ Workflows Run: 45                                      │
│                                                        │
│ Trend: ▁▂▃▅▇▆▄ (tokens/day)                           │
│        ↑15% vs previous week                           │
╰────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

### wa analytics breakdown
```bash
wa analytics breakdown [--by agent|account|workflow] [--period 7d|30d|90d]

# By agent
Agent         Tokens      Cost     % of Total
Codex         856,000     $8.56    69%
Claude Code   378,567     $3.78    31%

# By workflow
Workflow              Runs  Avg Tokens  Total Cost
handle_usage_limits   32    5,000       $1.60
handle_compaction     13    2,500       $0.33
```

### wa analytics trend
```bash
wa analytics trend [--metric tokens|cost|rate_limits] [--period 7d|30d]

# Output (sparkline chart)
Tokens per Day (Last 30 Days)
▁▂▂▃▃▄▅▅▆▆▇▇█▇▇▆▆▅▅▄▄▃▃▂▂▁▁▁▁▁
Jan 1                            Jan 30
Min: 10,000  Max: 100,000  Avg: 45,000
```

### wa analytics export
```bash
wa analytics export [--format csv|json] [--period 7d|30d|90d] [--output file]

# Examples
wa analytics export --format csv --period 30d --output usage.csv
wa analytics export --format json --period 7d
```

### wa analytics alerts
```bash
wa analytics alerts [--list|--add|--remove]

# List alerts
ID    Metric     Threshold   Status
1     cost       $50/day     OK
2     tokens     1M/day      TRIGGERED (1.2M)

# Add alert
wa analytics alerts --add --metric cost --threshold 100 --period day
```

```bash
wa robot analytics breakdown --by agent
wa robot analytics export --format json
```

 {"id":"wa-985.4","title":"Proactive alerts: threshold monitoring and notification triggers","description":"# Proactive alerts

## Purpose
Alert users before they hit limits or exceed budgets, preventing surprises and enabling proactive optimization.

## Alert Types

### 1. Cost Threshold Alerts
```rust
pub struct CostAlert {
    id: AlertId,
    threshold: Decimal,      // e.g., $50.00
    period: AlertPeriod,     // Day, Week, Month
    current_value: Decimal,
    percent_of_threshold: f32,
}

pub enum AlertLevel {
    Info,      // 50% of threshold
    Warning,   // 75% of threshold
    Critical,  // 90% of threshold
    Exceeded,  // 100%+ of threshold
}
```

### 2. Token Usage Alerts
```rust
pub struct TokenAlert {
    threshold: u64,
    period: AlertPeriod,
    per_agent: Option<AgentType>,
}
```

### 3. Rate Limit Frequency Alerts
```rust
pub struct RateLimitAlert {
    max_hits: u32,     // e.g., 5 per hour
    period: Duration,
}
```

### 4. Account Balance Alerts
```rust
pub struct AccountBalanceAlert {
    account_id: String,
    min_percent_remaining: f32,  // e.g., 15%
}
```

## Alert Configuration
```toml
# wa.toml
[analytics.alerts]
enabled = true

[[analytics.alerts.rules]]
metric = "cost"
threshold = 50.0
period = "day"
notify = ["terminal"]  # or "webhook", "email"

[[analytics.alerts.rules]]
metric = "account_balance"
threshold = 15  # percent
account = "*"   # all accounts
notify = ["terminal", "webhook"]
```

## Alert Monitoring Service
```rust
pub struct AlertMonitor {
    rules: Vec<AlertRule>,
    storage: StorageHandle,
    notifiers: Vec<Box<dyn Notifier>>,
}

impl AlertMonitor {
    pub async fn check_alerts(&self) -> Vec<TriggeredAlert> {
        let mut triggered = vec![];
        
        for rule in &self.rules {
            let current = self.get_current_value(&rule).await?;
            if let Some(alert) = rule.check(current) {
                triggered.push(alert);
            }
        }
        
        triggered
    }
    
    pub async fn notify_triggered(&self, alerts: &[TriggeredAlert]) -> Result<()> {
        for alert in alerts {
            for notifier in &self.notifiers {
                notifier.notify(alert).await?;
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

-{"id":"wa-9dp","title":"Tiered update rates — aggressive backoff for idle and background panes","description":"## Goal
Implement intelligent tiered polling rates for pane output capture, aggressively reducing polling frequency for idle and background panes while maintaining responsive capture for active panes.

## Background & Motivation
wa's CaptureScheduler (tailer.rs) currently polls all panes at a uniform rate. With 50+ panes, this means 250+ mux protocol requests per second, even when most panes are idle (waiting for rate limits, thinking, etc.). This wastes CPU, network, and mux server capacity.

AI agent workloads have a distinctive pattern: at any given time, maybe 5-10 panes are actively producing output while the other 40+ are idle. wa already has pattern detection that identifies pane states (idle, rate-limited, thinking, active). We should use this to dramatically reduce polling for non-active panes.

## Relationship to Other Optimizations
This bead works in concert with two complementary beads:
- **wa-x4rq (Notification Coalescing)**: wa-9dp sets WHEN to poll each pane. wa-x4rq batches the NOTIFICATIONS that trigger those polls. Together: wa-9dp reduces baseline poll rate by 80-90%, and wa-x4rq further reduces redundant captures within each tier's interval.
- **wa-3bin (Smart Priority)**: wa-9dp implements the mechanical tier system. wa-3bin provides the CLASSIFICATION that maps panes to tiers (using pattern detection + output rates). wa-9dp can work standalone with simple time-based classification, but wa-3bin makes it much smarter.
- **backpressure.rs (existing)**: Backpressure's tiers (Green/Yellow/Red/Black) are about SYSTEM LOAD. bd-9dp's tiers are about PANE ACTIVITY. They're orthogonal: under backpressure (Red/Black), bd-9dp's intervals can be further multiplied to reduce load.

## Technical Design

### Tier Definitions
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/tailer.rs (modify existing CaptureScheduler)

pub enum PaneTier {
    Active,      // Producing output -- poll every 200ms (current default)
    Thinking,    // Agent is processing -- poll every 2s
    Idle,        // No output for >30s -- poll every 5s
    Background,  // Minimized/hidden tab -- poll every 10s
    Dormant,     // Rate-limited or paused >5min -- poll every 30s
}

impl PaneTier {
    pub fn poll_interval(&self) -> Duration {
        match self {
            Self::Active => Duration::from_millis(200),
            Self::Thinking => Duration::from_secs(2),
            Self::Idle => Duration::from_secs(5),
            Self::Background => Duration::from_secs(10),
            Self::Dormant => Duration::from_secs(30),
        }
    }

    /// Under backpressure, multiply intervals by this factor
    pub fn backpressure_multiplier(&self, tier: BackpressureTier) -> f64 {
        match tier {
            BackpressureTier::Green => 1.0,
            BackpressureTier::Yellow => 1.5,
            BackpressureTier::Red => 3.0,
            BackpressureTier::Black => 10.0,
        }
    }
}
```

### Tier Classification Logic
Simple version (standalone, without wa-3bin):
```rust
fn classify_pane(&self, pane_id: PaneId) -> PaneTier {
    let last_output = self.last_output_time(pane_id);
    let elapsed = Instant::now() - last_output;
    let state = self.pattern_engine.current_state(pane_id);

    match state {
        AgentState::RateLimited => PaneTier::Dormant,
        AgentState::WaitingForInput if elapsed > Duration::from_secs(300) => PaneTier::Dormant,
        AgentState::WaitingForInput => PaneTier::Idle,
        AgentState::Thinking => PaneTier::Thinking,
        _ if elapsed > Duration::from_secs(30) => PaneTier::Idle,
        _ => PaneTier::Active,
    }
}
```

With wa-3bin (enhanced classification):
```rust
fn classify_pane_enhanced(&self, pane_id: PaneId, classifier: &PriorityClassifier) -> PaneTier {
    match classifier.classify(pane_id) {
        PanePriority::Critical | PanePriority::High => PaneTier::Active,
        PanePriority::Medium => PaneTier::Thinking,
        PanePriority::Low => PaneTier::Idle,
        PanePriority::Background => PaneTier::Dormant,
    }
}
```

### Instant Promotion
When a pane produces new output, it's immediately promoted to Active tier:
```rust
fn on_pane_output(&mut self, pane_id: PaneId) {
    self.tier_map.insert(pane_id, PaneTier::Active);
    self.last_output_time.insert(pane_id, Instant::now());
    self.wake_scheduler.notify_one();
}
```

### Integration with CaptureScheduler
```rust
async fn capture_loop(&mut self) {
    loop {
        let next = self.pane_timers.iter()
            .min_by_key(|(_, timer)| timer.deadline())
            .map(|(id, _)| *id);

        if let Some(pane_id) = next {
            let timer = &self.pane_timers[&pane_id];
            tokio::select! {
                _ = timer.tick() => {
                    self.capture_pane(pane_id).await;
                    let tier = self.classify_pane(pane_id);
                    // Apply backpressure multiplier
                    let bp_mult = tier.backpressure_multiplier(
                        self.backpressure.current_tier()
                    );
                    let interval = tier.poll_interval().mul_f64(bp_mult);
                    self.pane_timers.get_mut(&pane_id)
                        .unwrap()
                        .set_interval(interval);
                }
                _ = self.wake_scheduler.notified() => {
                    continue;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Configuration
```toml
[capture.tiers]
active_ms = 200
thinking_ms = 2000
idle_ms = 5000
background_ms = 10000
dormant_ms = 30000
idle_threshold_secs = 30
dormant_threshold_secs = 300
```

## Existing Code References
- CaptureScheduler: crates/wa-core/src/tailer.rs (adaptive polling, token bucket)
- TailerConfig: poll intervals, backoff settings
- BackpressureMonitor: crates/wa-core/src/backpressure.rs (system load tiers -- orthogonal to activity tiers)

## Expected Impact
- 80-90% reduction in mux protocol requests (from ~250/s to ~30/s for typical workload)
- Proportional CPU reduction on both wa and mux server
- Mux server lock contention dramatically reduced
- Active panes remain just as responsive

## Dependencies
None -- standalone improvement. Enhanced by wa-3bin (smart classification) and complemented by wa-x4rq (notification coalescing).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Panes classified into correct tiers based on output activity
- Active panes polled at 200ms (no regression)
- Idle panes polled at 5s (25x reduction)
- Dormant panes polled at 30s (150x reduction)
- Instant promotion to Active when output detected
- Backpressure multiplier applied on top of tier intervals
- Configuration via wa.toml
- Metrics: per-tier pane counts, total requests/s, backpressure multiplier

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation, 1 hour testing

## Benchmark & Property Testing Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add `benches/tiered_update.rs` using criterion:
- **tier_assignment_latency**: Benchmark classify_pane() for 50, 100, 200 panes. Target: <1us per pane classification (must be fast since it runs on every poll cycle).
- **tier_assignment_with_pattern_engine**: Benchmark classify_pane() when it queries the pattern engine for AgentState. Measure the overhead of pattern engine lookups.
- **scheduler_tick_overhead**: Benchmark a full capture_loop tick (select next pane, classify, update timer) without actual mux I/O. Target: <10us per tick for 100 panes.
- **instant_promotion_latency**: Benchmark the time from on_pane_output() call to the pane being reclassified as Active. Target: <5us.

### Proptest
Add `tests/proptest_tiers.rs`:
- **tier_transition_invariants**: For any arbitrary sequence of (pane_id, elapsed_time, agent_state) tuples (proptest generates), assert that tier transitions are monotonically correct: Active -> Thinking -> Idle -> Dormant as elapsed time increases (never skipping tiers or going backwards without new output).
- **backpressure_multiplier_monotonicity**: For any PaneTier and any BackpressureTier, the effective interval (tier.poll_interval * backpressure_multiplier) must be monotonically non-decreasing as backpressure increases (Green <= Yellow <= Red <= Black).
- **instant_promotion_always_works**: For any pane in any tier, calling on_pane_output() must result in PaneTier::Active on the next classify_pane() call.

## Cross-References
- **wa-3bin** (Smart pane priority classification): wa-3bin provides the intelligent PanePriority classifier that feeds into bd-9dp's tier system via classify_pane_enhanced(). When wa-3bin is implemented, wa-9dp should switch from time-based classification to priority-based classification for more accurate tier assignment.
- **wa-283h4.8** (Entropy-aware scheduling): The entropy-aware scheduler uses information-theoretic measures to decide which panes have the most "surprising" (high-entropy) output.     match classifier.classify(pane_id) {
```rust
        PanePriority::Critical | PanePriority::High => PaneTier::Active,
        PanePriority::Medium => PaneTier::Thinking,
        PanePriority::Low => PaneTier::Idle,
        PanePriority::Background => PaneTier::Dormant,
    }
}
```

### Instant Promotion
When a pane produces new output, it's immediately promoted to Active tier:
```rust
fn on_pane_output(&mut self, pane_id: PaneId) {
    self.tier_map.insert(pane_id, PaneTier::Active);
    self.last_output_time.insert(pane_id, Instant::now());
    self.wake_scheduler.notify_one();
}
```

### Integration with CaptureScheduler
```rust
async fn capture_loop(&mut self) {
    loop {
        let next = self.pane_timers.iter()
            .min_by_key(|(_, timer)| timer.deadline())
            .map(|(id, _)| *id);

        if let Some(pane_id) = next {
            let timer = &self.pane_timers[&pane_id];
            tokio::select! {
                _ = timer.tick() => {
                    self.capture_pane(pane_id).await;
                    let tier = self.classify_pane(pane_id);
                    // Apply backpressure multiplier
                    let bp_mult = tier.backpressure_multiplier(
                        self.backpressure.current_tier()
                    );
                    let interval = tier.poll_interval().mul_f64(bp_mult);
                    self.pane_timers.get_mut(&pane_id)
                        .unwrap()
                        .set_interval(interval);
                }
                _ = self.wake_scheduler.notified() => {
                    continue;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Configuration
```toml
[capture.tiers]
active_ms = 200
thinking_ms = 2000
idle_ms = 5000
background_ms = 10000
dormant_ms = 30000
idle_threshold_secs = 30
dormant_threshold_secs = 300
```

- CaptureScheduler: crates/wa-core/src/tailer.rs (adaptive polling, token bucket)
- TailerConfig: poll intervals, backoff settings
- BackpressureMonitor: crates/wa-core/src/backpressure.rs (system load tiers -- orthogonal to activity tiers)

- 80-90% reduction in mux protocol requests (from ~250/s to ~30/s for typical workload)
- Proportional CPU reduction on both wa and mux server
- Mux server lock contention dramatically reduced
- Active panes remain just as responsive

## Dependencies
None -- standalone improvement. Enhanced by wa-3bin (smart classification) and complemented by wa-x4rq (notification coalescing).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Panes classified into correct tiers based on output activity
- Active panes polled at 200ms (no regression)

- Backpressure multiplier applied on top of tier intervals

 {"id":"wa-9i3rh","title":"File descriptor management and resource pre-allocation for 200+ panes","description":"# File Descriptor and Resource Management

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization

## Problem
200 panes × 25 FDs/pane = 5000+ FDs. Linux default is often 1024/process. FD exhaustion causes cryptic "Too many open files" errors that crash agents silently.

## Solution

### 1. Startup resource validation
On FrankenTerm launch, validate and configure:
- **Linux**: fs.file-max >= 2097152, ulimit -n >= 65536, inotify max_user_watches >= 524288
- **macOS**: kern.maxfiles >= 65536, kern.maxfilesperproc >= 65536, launchctl limit maxfiles
- Warn if insufficient and provide platform-specific fix commands

### 2. FD budget tracking
- Per-pane FD counter (pipes: 3, PTY: 2, sockets: variable)
- Global FD watermark with configurable high/low thresholds
- Alert at 80% of limit, refuse new panes at 95%
- **Linux**: Audit via /proc/self/fd
- **macOS**: Audit via proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDLISTFDS)

### 3. FD leak detection
- Periodic FD audit (platform-specific, see above)
- Detect FDs that grow monotonically (leak signature)
- Report pane-correlated FD leaks

### 4. Socket connection pooling
- Reuse Unix socket connections to mux server (already in progress: bd-41w)
- HTTP connection keep-alive for API calls
- Limit concurrent outbound connections per pane

## Tests
- Start 200 panes, verify FD count stays within budget
- Simulate FD leak, verify detection within 60s
- Verify startup validation catches low ulimit (test both Linux and macOS paths)
- **Use criterion benchmarks for FD audit latency**

## Acceptance criteria
- FrankenTerm validates resource limits on startup (Linux + macOS)
- FD budget tracked per-pane and globally
- FD leaks detected and reported
- No "Too many open files" under 200-pane load","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:11:05.388588Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T23:24:44.451567-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T23:24:44.451567-05:00","close_reason":"FD budget tracking, resource validation, leak detection, 200-pane admission control with 19 tests"}
 {"id":"wa-9l5","title":"Scheduled backup system: cron-style automatic backups with retention","description":"# Task: Scheduled Backup System

## Goal
Provide peace of mind through automatic, hands-off backups.

## Configuration
```toml
[backup.scheduled]
enabled = true

# Schedule (cron-like or simple keywords)
schedule = "daily"  # or "0 3 * * *" for 3 AM daily

# Retention policy

# Destination

# Options
compress = true
metadata_only = false  # Set true for quick backups

# Notifications
notify_on_failure = true
notify_on_success = false
```

## Behavior
- Backup runs as part of `wa watch` (if enabled)
- Uses same export logic as `wa backup export`
- Rotation: delete oldest when max_backups exceeded
- Retry: attempt 3 times with exponential backoff on failure

  ```
    Last backup: 2026-01-18T03:00:15 (45 MB)
    Next backup: 2026-01-19T03:00:00
    Backups kept: 8/10
  ```

## Implementation Notes
- Use tokio cron scheduler or simple timer
- Lock coordination: brief pause of writes during export
- Fail gracefully: never crash watcher if backup fails

## Testing
- Unit tests: schedule parsing, rotation logic
- Integration: trigger scheduled backup, verify rotation
- E2E: run watcher with backup enabled, verify files created

## Acceptance Criteria
- Backup runs on configured schedule
- Rotation respects both retention_days and max_backups
- Status shows last/next backup times
- Failures logged and optionally notified

 {"id":"wa-9lh","title":"E2E script: quick-fix suggestions (events + errors → actionable commands)","description":"# E2E script: quick-fix suggestions (events + errors → actionable commands)

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that wa always answers the user’s “now what?” question:
- **Unhandled events** include actionable suggestions (quick fix + preview + manual).
- **Errors** include actionable remediation (with context-aware hints).
- Suggestions are **safe, copy-pasteable**, and **machine-usable** (when requested via JSON).

This is the integration validation for the quick-fix system (`wa-bnm`) under the standard E2E harness.

## Why this matters
Quick-fix suggestions are a UX multiplier:
- They reduce cognitive load (“what command handles this?”).
- They teach the system (users learn correct workflows).
- They enable agent automation (robots can pick the recommended remediation).

## Key constraints (robustness)
- **Deterministic**: no fixed `sleep N`. Synchronize via wait-for/quiescence with explicit timeouts.
- **No `eval` / no shell interpolation** when validating executability.
  - If we need to *run* a suggestion, we must do so via a structured argv array (no shell).
- **No transcript leakage**: artifacts must not contain raw pane scrollback; suggestions must be redacted/safe.
- Use the standard harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).

## Test setup (E2E harness)
- Start a WezTerm mux server and spawn deterministic “dummy agent panes” (harness-owned).
- Run `wa watch` in an isolated E2E workspace with:
  - a known pattern pack enabled
  - a known set of panes that will emit markers to create *unhandled* events
  - stable output mode for machine parsing (`--format json` where applicable)

## Data contract (strongly recommended)
To make suggestions both copy-friendly and robot-executable, JSON outputs that include suggestions should carry both:
- `display`: human copy/paste string (best effort)
- `argv`: machine-executable argv array (preferred; avoids shells)

Example shape (illustrative):
```json
{
  "suggestions": {
    "quick_fix": {"display": "wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3", "argv": ["wa","workflow","run","handle_compaction","--pane","3"]},
    "preview":   {"display": "wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3 --dry-run", "argv": ["wa","workflow","run","handle_compaction","--pane","3","--dry-run"]},
    "manual":    {"display": "Ask the agent to re-read AGENTS.md and key files"}
  }
}
```

If `argv` is not available in v1, then E2E should still validate that `display` is strictly in a safe subset (no pipes/redirects/command substitutions), and **must not attempt execution**.

## Scenarios

### 1) Event suggestions appear and are complete
- Trigger a known unhandled event deterministically (via dummy pane output + pattern match).
- Run: `wa events --unhandled --format json`.
- Assert for each returned event:
  - `quick_fix` exists and is relevant to the event (e.g., suggests the correct workflow)
  - `preview` exists (`--dry-run` variant)
  - `manual` exists (human explanation)

### 2) Error suggestions for invalid inputs
- Run an operation that must fail deterministically (no WezTerm state needed), e.g.:
  - `wa send --pane 999 "hello" --format json` (or equivalent command expected to error)
- Assert error output includes:
  - a clear primary error message
  - “available panes” (context)
  - “did you mean …” when there’s a near match
  - suggested next steps (e.g., `wa status`)

### 3) Policy denial suggestions (context-aware)
- Create a deterministic unsafe pane state (fixture / dummy alt-screen mode).
- Attempt a send (`wa send …`) and assert the denial output includes:
  - the *reason* (e.g., AltScreen)
  - the *quick fix* (exit AltScreen)
  - the “why” trace command (e.g., `wa why denied --pane <id>`)

### 4) Fuzzy matching and typo recovery
- Trigger “pane not found” with a close pane id and assert “did you mean” suggestion.
- Trigger “unknown workflow” with a close name and assert fuzzy match suggestion.

### 5) Suggestion executability (no shell)
Pick one event suggestion that is expected to be a pure `wa …` remediation:
- Extract `suggestions.quick_fix.argv` (preferred).
- Execute it directly **without a shell** (argv exec).
- Assert the expected effect (e.g., a workflow execution is created OR a dry-run preview succeeds).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`) with prerequisites and default inclusion status.

## Artifacts
- `suggestions_output.json` (redacted event/error JSON)
- `suggestion_validation.json` (what we validated + pass/fail per rule)
- `copy_paste_execution.log` (argv executed + exit codes)
- `wa_watch.log`
- `events.jsonl`

## Logging contract
All steps log:
- case name + scenario name
- workspace + mux identifiers
- timeouts used
- extracted suggestion IDs (not full secrets)

Example:
```
[QUICKFIX_E2E] scenario=event_suggestions query=wa events --unhandled --format json
[QUICKFIX_E2E] event=session.compaction pane=3 quick_fix="wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3"
[QUICKFIX_E2E] scenario=exec argv=[wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3] rc=0
```

## Testing
- Determinism:
  - no fixed sleeps
  - every wait has a timeout and an error message pointing to artifacts
- Safety:
  - never execute suggestion strings via `eval`/`sh -c`
  - ensure no suggestion `display` contains shell metacharacters (`;`, `|`, `&&`, backticks, `$(`) unless explicitly allowed by a strict policy
- Privacy:
  - run the standard redaction scan (same logic as `wa-4vx.10.18`) over artifacts
  - assert suggestions do not contain secrets (device codes, tokens)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Unhandled events include quick_fix + preview + manual suggestions.
- [ ] Common errors include actionable fixes with context (available panes/workflows).
- [ ] Policy denials include remediation + why-trace hints.
- [ ] Executability is validated without shells (argv exec), or safely skipped if `argv` isn’t available yet.
- [ ] Artifacts/logs are sufficient to debug failures.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:13:11.641286474Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-30T05:49:21.228349238Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T05:49:21.228259141Z","close_reason":"Added quickfix_suggestions E2E scenario, registered in e2e_test registry, updated checklist; validated registry alignment.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10.11","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:32Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:32Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.6.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:32Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-9lh","depends_on_id":"wa-bnm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:32Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-w5l","title":"[Human command] `wa config export/import` for config-only migration and sharing","description":"# Task: wa config export/import

## Goal
Allow users to share configurations or migrate config between machines without data.

## Command Design
```bash
# Export current effective config
$ wa config export
# Saved to: ~/.config/wa/exports/wa_config_2026-01-18.toml

$ wa config export --output my-setup.toml

# Import and merge config
$ wa config import my-setup.toml
Importing configuration...
  [patterns] 3 new rules
  [notifications] webhook config updated
  [safety] policy unchanged

$ wa config import shared-team-config.toml --dry-run
Would update:
  [patterns.custom_rules] +3 rules
  [notifications.webhook.url] http://... → http://...new
  [safety.policy] no change

# Replace entirely
$ wa config import my-setup.toml --replace
⚠️  This will replace your entire configuration!
Backup saved to: ~/.config/wa/exports/backup_pre_import.toml
Continue? [y/N]
```

## Export Contents
```toml
# wa configuration export
# Exported: 2026-01-18T14:30:15Z
# wa version: 0.1.0

[patterns]
# Custom patterns defined by user
[[patterns.custom_rules]]
id = "my-error-pattern"
pattern = "FATAL:"
# ...

[notifications]
# User notification settings
# ...

[safety]
# Policy configuration
# ...

# Note: Secrets are NOT exported
# Webhook URLs with tokens are redacted: http://...?token=[REDACTED]
```

## Safety
- Never export secrets (API keys, tokens in URLs)
- Require confirmation for --replace
- Create backup before destructive import
- Validate config syntax before import

## Testing
- Unit tests: export format, merge logic, redaction
- Integration: export/import round-trip
- E2E: share config between two wa instances

## Acceptance Criteria
- Export creates valid, importable TOML
- Secrets are redacted in exports
- Import shows diff before applying
- Merge mode preserves user customizations

### Test Harness Library
```rust
// tests/e2e/harness/mod.rs
pub struct E2ETestHarness {
    wa_process: Option<Child>,
    test_db: TempDir,
    log_file: File,
    start_time: Instant,
}

impl E2ETestHarness {
    pub async fn new(test_name: &str) -> Result<Self> {
        let test_db = TempDir::new()?;
        let log_file = create_log_file(test_name)?;

        log_info!(&log_file, "=== Starting E2E Test: {} ===", test_name);

        Ok(Self {
            wa_process: None,
            test_db,
            log_file,
            start_time: Instant::now(),
        })
    }

    pub async fn start_wa(&mut self, args: &[&str]) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Starting wa with args: {:?}", args);

        let process = Command::new("target/release/wa")
            .args(args)
            .env("WA_DB_PATH", self.test_db.path())
            .env("WA_LOG_LEVEL", "debug")
            .spawn()?;

        self.wa_process = Some(process);

        // Wait for ready
        self.wait_for_ready().await?;
        log_info!(&self.log_file, "wa started successfully");
        Ok(())
    }

    pub async fn inject_output(&self, pane_id: u32, text: &str) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Injecting output to pane {}: {:?}", pane_id, text);
        // Mock or real injection
    }

    pub async fn wait_for_event(&self, rule_id: &str, timeout: Duration) -> Result<Event> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Waiting for event: {} (timeout: {:?})", rule_id, timeout);
        // Poll for event
    }

    pub async fn assert_db_contains(&self, query: &str) -> Result<()> {
        log_debug!(&self.log_file, "Checking DB for: {}", query);
        // Query and assert
    }
}

impl Drop for E2ETestHarness {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if let Some(mut process) = self.wa_process.take() {
            let _ = process.kill();
        }
        let elapsed = self.start_time.elapsed();
        log_info!(&self.log_file, "=== E2E Test completed ({:.2}s) ===", elapsed.as_secs_f64());
    }
}
```

### Detailed Logging
```rust
// tests/e2e/harness/logging.rs
use std::fs::File;
use std::io::Write;
use chrono::Utc;

pub fn create_log_file(test_name: &str) -> Result<File> {
    let timestamp = Utc::now().format("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S");
    let path = format!("tests/e2e/logs/{}_{}.log", test_name, timestamp);
    std::fs::create_dir_all("tests/e2e/logs")?;
    File::create(path)
}

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! log_info {
    ($file:expr, $($arg:tt)*) => {{
        let msg = format!($($arg)*);
        let timestamp = chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%.3fZ");
        let line = format!("[{}] [INFO] {}\n", timestamp, msg);
        let _ = $file.write_all(line.as_bytes());
        eprintln!("{}", line.trim());
    }};
}

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! log_debug {
    ($file:expr, $($arg:tt)*) => {{
        let msg = format!($($arg)*);
        let timestamp = chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%.3fZ");
        let line = format!("[{}] [DEBUG] {}\n", timestamp, msg);
        let _ = $file.write_all(line.as_bytes());
        if std::env::var("E2E_LOG_LEVEL").map(|l| l == "debug").unwrap_or(false) {
            eprintln!("{}", line.trim());
        }
    }};
}

#[macro_export]
macro_rules! log_error {
    ($file:expr, $($arg:tt)*) => {{
        let msg = format!($($arg)*);
        let timestamp = chrono::Utc::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%.3fZ");
        let line = format!("[{}] [ERROR] {}\n", timestamp, msg);
        let _ = $file.write_all(line.as_bytes());
        eprintln!("\x1b[31m{}\x1b[0m", line.trim());
    }};
}
```
